3. Moreover, we recommended that Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards and ARE be reconciled with the annual RPCPPE.

8. We recommended that Management fast track the reconciliation of records with the GSIS. Further, we recommended that Management recognize a “provision” for the outcome of the reconciliation in the amount as can be reliably estimated and appropriate fund to address eventual payment thereof.
12. Submit the List of PPAs (complete with necessary details) for audit at the beginning of every year;

14. Inform the concerned audit team (within 10-day after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity) that the signboards / public notices are already posted (for validation purposes); and

16. We recommended that Management ensure that a copy of contract documents (such as memorandum of agreement, purchase order and supporting documents) be submitted for contract review within the prescribed 5-day period from the execution / issuance of the contract pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001.

17. We recommended that Management notify the COA to witness any inspection and to validate any monthly monitoring report of Management on the status of projects pursuant to Section 6.6 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and Item 3.2 COA of Circular No. 2013-004, respectively.
19. We recommended that Municipal Accountant review the other receivables account, effect pertinent correction in the books, if any, and disclose the matter in the financial statements.

26. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the nature and details of the dormant accounts “deferred credits / other unearned income” and “other payables”, effect any possible corrections in the books and or disclose in the ensuing financial statements all necessary information relative thereto.

1.9 We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to complete the physical count
of all types of PPE duly witnessed by a COA representative and
prepare the prescribed annual RPCPPE thereon properly
reconciled with the PPE ledger cards and other pertinent records
including property cards and AREs; and
b. Submit RPCPPE for audit.

2.8 We recommended that the Honorable Mayor cause all concerned
personnel to comply with the following:
a. Immediately submit reports and documents relating to the fund
transfer to the Office of the Auditor assigned to the implementing
LGU including RCIs, RoDs duly supported with paid DVs/paid
payrolls complete with supporting documents;
b. Keep a separate updated subsidiary records for each transfer
whether or not a separate bank account is maintained;
c. Record the receipt and utilizations of the fund transfer and or
effect necessary adjustments/corrections/disclosures in the books
and reports; and
d. Submit to the Office of the Auditor a schedule of
transfers/subsidies actually received by the LGU including the
respective bank accounts being maintained therefor, if any, so to
facilitate proper audit thereto.

3.8 We recommended that Management fast track the reconciliation of
records with the GSIS. Further, recognize a “provision” for the
outcome of the reconciliation in the amount as could be sufficiently and
reliably estimated.

7.7 We recommended that Management cause to submit on time the GPB
and the annual GAD AR pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular
No. 2014-001 and PCW-NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01.

10.7 We recommended that settlement of the suspension of disbursements
totaling ₱1,931,732.73 be made within the 90 calendar days from
receipt of the notice of suspension. Further, direct effective
disbursement controls be installed to ensure that disbursements are
duly supported with all necessary documents.
1. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee possessing competence to conduct physical count of PPE.

4. We recommended that Management create a SAGF for the BGCM and all other future funds of similar nature pursuant to Section 313 of RA No. 7160, Sections 105 and 107 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs and Item No. 3.7 of DBM-LBC No. 125 to facilitate easy monitoring / tracking of the utilization of the funds.
5. We recommended that Management prepare the monthly BGCM Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of PPAs pursuant to DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125.
6. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant attach paid DVs/payroll and other JEV requiring source document for submission for audit.
7. We recommended that Management ensure timely submission of monthly reports for audit such as RoD, RCI, RCD, CRAAF and BRS together with their supporting documents.

9. We recommended that Management ensure that concerned personnel are kept abreast of the guidelines for disbursements and monitoring of DRRMF as prescribed under Circular 2012-002. Specifically, we recommend that Management ensure timely rendition of monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF and proper disbursements of LDRRMF-QRF duly supported with a Local Sanggunian resolution and a declaration of a state of calamity.

10. We recommended that Management cause to submit on time the GAD Plan and Budget and the annual GAD Accomplishment Report pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2014-001 and PCWNEDA- DBM JC 2012-01.
11. We recommended that Management designate a focal person tasked to ensure proper publicity and monitoring of PPAs pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-004.
13. Post the information in the List of PPAs in appropriate places using the prescribed sizes of signboards / public notices;
15. Maintain records on the Project Status of PPAs (updated on the basis of the prescribed monthly Monitoring Report of PPAs verified by the COA TAS).

18. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer review the beginning balance of the SCBAA for the year ended December 31, 2020, effect necessary corrections in the books, if any, and disclose necessary information pertinent to the beginning balance of the SCBAA in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

20. We recommended that Municipal Accountant review and effect necessary corrections in the books concerning the transfer of LDRRM fund of ₱360,000.00 for CY 2020 to the LDRRM – special trust fund which did not reconcile with assertion per Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amount that the whole LDRRM fund of ₱4,019,557.95 for CY 2020 was actually utilized in full in the same year.

21. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant identify the nature of the prior period errors account, effect necessary corrections, if any, in the books and always ensure disclosures of the relevant information in the Notes to Financial Statements to give clarity to the account.

22. We recommended that Municipal Accountant ensure proper classification of accounts to ensure consistency of entries in the financial statements. Specifically, review and effect necessary corrections in the recording of the alleged subsidy to other funds of ₱34,400.00 where the supposed corresponding subsidy from other funds account used to capture the subsidy either in the special education fund or trust fund does not exist.

23. We recommended that Municipal Accountant reconcile the MOOE per SCBAA and MOOE per SFPe, effect necessary corrections in the books, if any, and ensure that the actual amounts of the SCBAA and the SFPe are always reconcilable for future statements.

24. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the prescribed disbursement procedures are strictly followed to enable timely rendition of disbursement reports pursuant to Sections 9, 44, and 49, Volume 1, of the NGAS for LGUs. Specifically, we recommended that Management ensure that RCI and ROD including supporting vouchers / payrolls are submitted to the COA not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month pursuant to Section 100 of PD No. 1445.

25. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all completed trust projects be transferred to the books of accounts for General Fund, if warranted, pursuant to the existing accounting rules.

1. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee composing of personnel possessing competence to conduct physical count of PPE.
2. Further, we recommended that the same Committee conduct physical count of PPE annually to be reported on the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) for submission for audit not later than January 31 of each year. Moreover, we recommended that Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards and Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment be reconciled with the annual RPCPPE.

3. We recommended that concerned personnel are kept abreast of the guidelines for disbursements and monitoring of DRRMF as prescribed under Circular 2012-002. Specifically, we recommended that Management ensure timely rendition of monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF and proper disbursements of LDRRMF-QRF duly supported with a local SANGGUNIAN resolution and a declaration of a state of calamity.

9. We recommended that Management fast track the reconciliation of records with the GSIS. Further, we recommended that Management recognize a “provision” for the outcome of the reconciliation in the amount as can be reliably estimated and appropriate fund to address eventual payment thereof.

10. We recommended that Management ensure that a copy of contract documents (such as memorandum of agreement, purchase order and supporting documents) be submitted for contract review within the prescribed 5-day period from the execution / issuance of the contract pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001.

11. We recommended that Management notify the COA to witness any inspection and acceptance of procurements by Management or to validate any monthly monitoring report of Management on the status of projects pursuant to Section 6.6 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and Item 3.2 COA of Circular No. 2013-004, respectively.

13. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the prescribed disbursement procedures are strictly followed to enable timely rendition of disbursement reports pursuant to Sections 9, 44, and 49, Volume 1, of the NGAS for LGUs. Specifically, we recommended that Management ensure that RCI and ROD including supporting vouchers/payrolls are submitted to the COA not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month pursuant to Section 100 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the nature and details of the dormant accounts “deferred credits / unearned income” and “other payables”, effect any pertinent possible corrections in the books and or disclose in the ensuing financial statements all necessary information relative thereto.

1.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory
Committee composing of personnel possessing competence to conduct
physical count of PPE. Further, we recommended that the same
Committee conduct physical count of PPE annually to be reported on
the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment
(RPCPPE) for submission for audit not later than January 31 of each
year. Moreover, we recommended that Property Cards, PPE Ledger
Cards and Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment be reconciled
with the annual RPCPPE.

2.9 We recommended that Management create a special account in the
general fund (SAGF) for the BGCM and all other future funds of
similar nature pursuant to Section 313 of RA No. 7160, Sections 105
and 107 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs and Item No. 3.7 of DBM-
LBC No. 125 to facilitate easy monitoring/tracking of the utilization of
the funds.

3.7 We recommended that Management preparethe monthly BGCM
Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of
Program/Project/Activity pursuant to DBM Local Budget Circular
No. 125.

4.6 We recommended that Municipal Accountant attach JEV to the paid
DV/payrolland other JEV requiring source document for submission
for audit.

5.7 We recommended that Management ensure timely submission of
monthly reports for audit such as Report of Disbursements (RoD),
Report of Checks Issued (RCI), Report of Collections and Deposits
(RCD), Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms
(CRAAF) and Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) together with
their supporting documents.

6.9 We recommended that Management fast track the reconciliation of
records with the GSIS. Further, we recommended that Management
recognize a “provision” for the outcome of the reconciliation in the
amount as can be reliably estimatedand appropriate fund to address
eventual payment thereof.

7.6 We recommended that Management ensure that concerned personnel
are kept abreast of the guidelines for disbursements and monitoring
of DRRMF as prescribed under Circular 2012-002. Specifically, we
recommend that Management ensure timely rendition of monthly
Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF and proper
disbursements of LDRRMF-QRF duly supported with a local
Sanggunian resolution and a declaration of a state of calamity.

8.7 We recommended that Management cause to submit on time the GAD
Plan and Budget and the annual GAD Accomplishment Report
pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2014-001 and PCW-
NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01.

9.6 We recommended that Management designate a focal person tasked
to ensure proper publicity and monitoring of PPAs pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2013-004. Specifically, we recommendedthat
Management:

a) Submit the List of PPAs (complete with necessary details) for
audit at the beginning of every year;
b) Post the information in the List of PPAs in appropriate places
using the prescribed sizes of signboards / public notices;
c) Inform the concerned audit team (within 10-day after the award of the
infrastructure project or before the start of the
program/activity) that the signboards / public notices are already
posted (for validation purposes); and
d) Maintain records on the Project Status of PPAs (updated on the
basis of the prescribed monthly Monitoring Report of PPAs
verified by the COA TAS).

10.7 We recommended that Management ensure that a copy of contract
documents (such as memorandum of agreement, purchase order and
supporting documents) be submitted for contract review within the
prescribed 5-day period from the execution / issuance of the contract
pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001.

11.9 We recommended that Management notify the COA to witness any
inspection and acceptance of procurements by Management or to
validate any monthly monitoring report of Management on the status
of projects pursuant to Section 6.6 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and
Item 3.2 COA of Circular No. 2013-004, respectively.

12.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Budget Officer review the beginning balance of the SCBAA for the
year ended December 31, 2020, effect necessary corrections in the
books, if any, and disclose necessary information pertinent to the
beginning balance of the SCBAA in the Notes to the Financial
Statements.

13.6 We recommended that Municipal Accountant review the other
receivables account, effect pertinent correction in the books, if any,
and disclose the matter in the financial statements.

14.10 We recommended that Municipal Accountant review and effect
necessary corrections in the booksconcerning the transfer of unspent
LDRRM fund of ₱360,000.00 for CY 2020 to the LDRRM – special
trust fund per Statement of Financial Performance which did not
reconcile with the assertion per Statement of Comparison of Budget
and Actual Amount that the whole LDRRM fund of ₱4,019,557.95 for
CY 2020 was actually utilized in full in the same year.

15.11 Werecommended that Municipal Accountant identify the nature of
the prior period errors account, effect necessary corrections, if any, in
the books and always ensure disclosures of relevant information in the
Notes to Financial Statements to give clarity to the account.

16.7 We recommended that Municipal Accountant ensure proper
classification of accounts to ensure consistency of entries in the
financial statements. Specifically, review and effect necessary
corrections in the recording of the alleged subsidy to other funds of
₱34,400.00 where the supposed corresponding subsidy from other
funds account used to capture the subsidy either in the special
education fund or trust fund does not exist.

17.6 We recommended that Municipal Accountant reconcile the MOOE
per SCBAA and MOOE per SFP, effect necessary corrections in the
books, if any, and ensure that the actual amounts of the SCBAA and
the SFP are always reconcilable for future statements.

18.7 We recommended that settlement of the suspension of disbursements
totaling ₱2,954,458.35be made within the 90 calendar days from
receipt of the notice of suspension. Further, we recommended that
effective disbursement controls be installed to ensure that
disbursements are duly supported with all necessary documents.

12. We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the limits of grant and use of cash advances as defined under COA Circular 97-002. Specifically, we recommended that concerned personnel refrain from drawing of cash advances for procurements. Management may maintain a Petty Cash Fund to defray recurring petty operating expenses for a month.

14. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all completed trust projects be transferred to the books of accounts for General Fund, if warranted, pursuant to the existing accounting rules.
15. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure recognition of “subsidy to other funds” as “subsidy from other funds” in the SEF books.

16. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant identify the nature of the “prior period errors” account, effect necessary corrections, if any, in the books and always ensure disclosures of relevant information in the Notes to Financial Statements to give clarity to the account.
17. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly classify current and non-current portions of loans payable in the Statement of Financial Position.

19. We recommended that concerned personnel review the “actual amount of surplus / (deficit)” as reflected in the Statement of Comparison between Budget and Actual Amount (SCBAA) for correction of possible misstatement. Further, we recommended that the “actual amount of actual surplus / (deficit)”, not earmarked for specific purposes, be carried forward as “actual amount of beginning balance” to the ensuing SCBAA or be disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements if such amount is earmarked for specific purposes.

20. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure disclosure of the correct “Reconciliation of Net Cash Flows from Operating Activities to Surplus / Deficit” in the Notes to Financial Statements.

21. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant rectify the errors in recognizing fund transfers as “Assistance and Subsidy”, instead as “Inter-Agency Payables”. The errors can be corrected by a debit to Prior Period Errors and credits to “Due to NGAs” and Due to LGUs, as the case maybe, and debit the same “inter-agency payables” accounts upon liquidation of the funds. Further, we recommended that receipts of similar fund transfers in the future be recorded using the same “inter-agency payables” accounts.

22. We recommended that Management cause for the prompt submission of the required documents to support the disbursements in the sum of ₱1,095,412.00. Further, we recommended that Management ensure strict adherence to the prescribed disbursement procedures as prescribed under the New Government Accounting System for LGUs.

We recommended that Management ensure that monthly Pre-Closing Trial Balance duly supported by SAAOB, for both current and continuing appropriations, be submitted for audit not later than the twentieth (20th) day after the end of each month.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce rendition of monthly reports in particular to RCD and CRAAF. As enforcement measures, Management might consider employing or designating additional personnel for the proper discharge of collecting and disbursing functions including provision of proper training to ensure work competence.
the balance per stock card / property cards should always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit and other property records like Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE); and

the Municipal Treasurer [Mayor] should cause for the conduct of physical count of PPE annually and the preparation of the annual Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) which has to be submitted to the concerned Auditor not later than January 31 of each year. Ideally, an inventory committee may be created to conduct the count.
Strict adherence by it in the utilization of the DRRMF;
Rendering a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF, copy furnished the COA;
We recommended that Management enforce due compliance with the requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-004, specifically the following:

At the beginning of the year, the Supervising Auditor (SA) and the Audit Team Leader (ATL) should be provided with a list of all- on-going government projects/programs/activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year. The list shall include the project name; implementing unit, office or division if is not the agency as a whole; brief description of the PPA; contractor or suppliers, if any; mode of procurement; funding source; cost or approved budget; project duration including start and completion dates; and location;
Provide the ATL with a monthly “Project Status” based on the data of the monthly monitoring report of PPAs prepared by the Municipality; and
Inform the SA and the ATL within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices.
We recommended that the Municipality should fast track the reconciliation of records with the GSIS.

Further, the Municipality should determine and recognize a provision for its probable liability to the GSIS including the setting-up of pertinent equivalent appropriation in the budget to address eventual payment thereof.

We recommended that Management ensure proper recording of subsidies and transfers. Specifically, we recommended that the account “Subsidy from Other Funds (not Special Education Tax)” be used to recognize the receipt of the subsidy in the books for Special Education Fund.

Further, we recommended that Management revisit existing disbursement controls of the Municipality and install appropriate measures designed to ensure that no claim would be paid unless supported by valid attachments.
We recommended that completed projects should be taken out from the books of the Trust Fund and transferred the same to the books of the General Fund.
We recommended that Management ensure that contracts including supporting documents are submitted for COA review within the prescribed 5-day period reckoning from the perfection of the agreements.
In addition, management must ensure that monthly reports pertinent to the contracts are made available on time for post-audit (i.e. RCI, ROD, RCD, RAAF).
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from drawing additional cash advances prior to the settlement of previous advances.
We further recommended that the LGU should consider employing or designating additional disbursing officers to avoid multiple cash advances being drawn by a lone accountable officer.

We recommended that Management ensure that the concerned auditor would be given prior notification of inspections and acceptance of procurements including any stage of completion of any particular project.
We recommended that Management strictly enforce taxing and other-revenue-raising powers of the LGU as prescribed under the LGC of 1991.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce the operational mandates of the Office of the Municipal Assessor as regard the assessment of real property for the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to come up with a realistic tax valuation of real property for use as valid input in the books and serving as basis in enforcing collections of RPT and SEF receivables.
We recommended that Management strictly enforce timely rendition of financial reports.
We recommended that Management strictly enforce the keeping of a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: (a) journals, (b) ledgers and (c) registries.

17.5 We recommended that Management cause for the prompt submission
of the required documents to support the disbursements in the sum of
₱1,095,412.00. Further, we recommended that Management ensure

We recommended that Management strictly enforce the provisions of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs and the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS), in particular to Sections 114 and 124 [Volume 1] of the Manual and PPSAS which mandates the following:

The Municipal Accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for each category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details of the PPE account in the inventory control account in the general ledger;

The General Services Officer (GSO) or the Municipal Treasurer, as the case maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies and PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and,
We recommended that the Municipality should provide adequate insurance protection for its property, plant and equipment from the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS.

We recommended that the Municipality should form a its own Municipal Solid Waste Management Board; and Comply with all applicable rules and regulations of Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 to ensure protection of public health and environment.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the mandates of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 and RA 10121 (PDRRM), specifically on accounting and reporting of LDRRMF such as the following:
Proper preparation of LDRRMF Plan and Budget, copy furnished the COA;
Maintaining a separate Registry for Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for LDRRMF, namely, RAAO – for QRF, RAAO – for MOOE and RAAO – for CO;
Ensuring that all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE are transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” (Code 438) in the Trust Fund books.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce existing guidelines in the management of GAD Fund as prescribed under PCW-NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01 and ensure that concerned personnel are properly oriented on GAD budgeting, accounting and reporting. Specifically, ensure that the Audit Team is furnished on time a copy of the:
GAD Plan and Budget pursuant to Paragraph V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 as well as
annual GAD Accomplishment Report to facilitate timely rendition of an audit action.

We recommended that Management effect all necessary corrections, if any, and disclosures in the recording of transaction in the amount of ₱500,000.00 presented as a deduction from the Trust Fund using an account “Subsidies-Others”.

We recommended that Management ensure the transfer of all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” (Code 438). Specifically, we recommended that Management cause for the immediate correction in the recording of the ₱360,000.00 posted as “Transfers of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds” which was not taken up in the books for Trust Fund.

We recommended that persons responsible for the audit suspensions of disbursements in the sum of ₱618,484.33 be required to settle the same within the timeframe prescribed under COA Circular 2009-006.

1.9 We recommended that Management ensure that monthly Pre-closing
Trial Balance duly supported by a SAAOB, for both current and
continuing appropriations, be submitted for audit not later than the
twentieth (20th) day after the end of each month.

2.10 We recommended that Management strictly enforce rendition of
monthly reports in particular to RCD and CRAAF. As enforcement
measures, Management might consider employing or designating
additional personnel for the proper discharge of collecting and
disbursing functions including provision of proper training to ensure
work competence.

3.12 We recommended that Management strictly enforce the provisions of the
New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs and the
Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS), in particular to
Sections 114 and 124 of the Manual and PPSAS 17 which mandates the
following:
A. The Municipal Accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for
each category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details
of the PPE account in the inventory control account in the general
ledger;
B. The General Services Officer (GSO) or the Municipal Treasurer, as
the case maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for
supplies and PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the
same and, that, the balance per stock card / property cards should
always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit and
other property records like Acknowledgement Receipt for
Equipment (ARE); and
C. The Municipal Treasurer should cause for the conduct of physical
count of PPE annually and the preparation of the annual Report on
the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE)
which has to be submitted to the concerned Auditor not later than
January 31 of each year. Ideally, an inventory committee may be
created to conduct the count.

4.5 We recommended that the Municipality should provide adequate
insurance protection for its property, plant and equipment from the
General Insurance Fund of the GSIS.

5.6 We recommended that the Municipality should:
A. Form a its own Municipal Solid Waste Management Board; and
B. Comply with all applicable rules and regulations of Ecological Solid
Waste Management Act of 2000 to ensure protection of public health
and environment.

6.10 We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the mandates of
COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, COA Circular 2014-
002 dated April 15, 2014 and RA 10121 (PDRRM), specifically on
accounting and reporting of LDRRMF such as the following:
A. Proper preparation of LDRRMF Plan and strict adherence by it in
the utilization of the DRRMF, copy furnished the COA;
B. Maintaining a separate Registry for Appropriations, Allotments and
Obligations (RAAO) for LDRRMF, namely, RAAO – for QRF,
RAAO – for MOOE and RAAO – for CO;
C. Rendering a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
LDRRMF, copy furnished the COA; and
D. Ensuring that all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and
LDRRMF-MOOE are transferred to the Special Trust Fund under
the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” (Code 438) in the Trust
Funds books.

7.5 We recommended that Management enforce due compliance with the
requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-004, specifically
the following:
A. At the beginning of the year, the Supervising Auditor (SA) and the
Audit Team Leader (ATL) should be provided with a list of all- on-
going government projects/programs/activities (PPAs) and those that
are to be implemented during the year. The list shall include the
project name; implementing unit, office or division if is not the
agency as a whole; brief description of the PPA; contractor or
suppliers, if any; mode of procurement; funding source; cost or
approved budget; project duration including start and completion
dates; and location;
B. Provide the ATL with a monthly “Project Status” based on the data
of the monthly monitoring report of PPAs prepared by the
Municipality; and
C. Inform the SA and the ATL within ten (10) days after the award of
the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity
that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices.

8.9 We recommended that Management strictly enforce existing guidelines
in the management of GAD Fund as prescribed under PCW-NEDA-
DBM JC 2012-01 and ensure that concerned personnel are properly
oriented on GAD budgeting, accounting and reporting. Specifically, we
recommended that Management ensure proper formulation of a GAD
Plan and Budget, timely rendition of an annual GAD Accomplishment
Report as well as proper keeping and recording of GAD transactions
including timely submission of all pertinent periodic reports for audit.

9.8 We recommended that the Municipality should fast track the
reconciliation of records with the GSIS. Further, the Municipality
should determine and recognize a provision for its probable liability to
the GSIS including the setting-up of pertinent equivalent appropriation
in the budget to address eventual payment thereof.

10.5 We recommended that Management ensure proper recording of
subsidies and transfers. Specifically, we recommended that all affected
accounts in the recording of Subsidy to Other Funds account in the
amount of ₱161,438.90 be corrected in the books.

11.5 We recommended that Management effect all necessary corrections and
disclosures in the recording of transaction in the amount of ₱500,000.00
presented as a deduction from the Trust Fund using an account
“Subsidies-Others”.

12.6 We recommended that Management ensure the transfer of all
unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE to
the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM”
(Code 438). Specifically, we recommended that Management cause for
the immediate correction in the recording of the ₱360,000.00 posted as
“Transfers of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds”
per Note 13 of the Notes to Financial Statements.

13.7 We recommended that persons responsible for the audit suspensions of
disbursements in the sum of ₱618,484.33 be required to settle the same
within the timeframe prescribed under COA Circular 2009-006.
Further, we recommended that Management revisit existing
disbursement controls of the Municipality and install appropriate
measures designed to ensure that no claim would be paid unless
supported by valid attachments.

We recommended that the LGU should strictly enforce the provisions of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs and the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS), in particular to Sections 114 and 124 of the Manual and PPSAS 17 which mandates the following: a) The accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for each category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details of the PPE account in the inventory control account in the general ledger; b) the general services officer (GSO) or the municipal treasurer, as the case maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies and PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and, that,, the balance per stock card / property cards should always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit and other property records like Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE); c) the local chief executive should cause for the conduct of physical count of PPE annually and the preparation of the annual Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) which has to be submitted to the concerned Auditor not later than January 31 of each year; and d) infrastructure assets should be recognized in the books of accounts as PPE so that assets recorded in the registry of public infrastructures should then be brought back to the books to starting the year 2016.
observations.managementAction: AAR The LGU was able to render a RPCPPE as of May 31, 2018.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant and the GSO failed to update their PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation of records between the LGU and the GSIS. Further, the LGU should set-up a provision with corresponding appropriation equal to the probable balance of actual payables after reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution.
observations.managementAction: AAR Pending completion of the reconciliation process, Management planned to consider the setting up of a provision of probable liability equal to the probable balance after reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution.
observations.reason: No visible attempt to expedite the reconciliation of accounts with the GSIS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by: A. demonstrating leadership by example; B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and C. upholding due compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the rendition of financial reports (e.g., Reports of Collections and Deposits (RCD), Consolidated Reports of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF), Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS), Reports of Checks Issued (RCI) and Reports of Disbursements (RoD)).
observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, the LGU was able to submit nearly on time its monthly Reports of RCD, CRAAF and BRS.
observations.reason: The LGU still failed to render on time other monthly reports, namely: RCD, CRAAF and BRS. Rendition of these reports might not have been strictly enforced because the reports seemed to have less importance to management given heavy reliance on IRA; also both collecting and disbursing functions were being discharged by the Municipal Treasurer alone, making timely preparation difficult.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation requiring top management to strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal accountability by: A. demonstrating leadership by example; B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and C. maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: a. journals, b. ledgers, c. registries, and d. cash records.
observations.managementAction: The LGU was able to fairly comply with necessary reforms A to C.
observations.reason: Other records such as ledger cards and property cards, and the books of accounts, registries and cashbooks were not kept updated on time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that completed projects should be taken out from the books
of the Trust Fund and transferred the same to the books of the General Fund.

We recommended that Top Management strictly enforce the rendition of monthly reports
in particular to RCD and CRAAF. As enforcement measures, Management might
consider employing or designating additional personnel for the proper discharge of
collecting and disbursing functions including provision of proper training to ensure work
competence.

We recommended that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation of records
with the GSIS. Further, the LGU should determine and recognize its probable payables
to the GSIS including the setting-up of pertinent equivalent appropriation in the budget to
address eventual payment thereof.

We recommended that the LGU should strictly enforce the provisions of the New
Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs and the Philippine
Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS), in particular to Sections 114 and 124
of the Manual and PPSAS 17 which mandates the following:
a) the Municipal Accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for each
category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details of the PPE
account in the inventory control account in the general ledger;
b) the General Services Officer (GSO) or the Municipal Treasurer, as the case
maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies and
PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and, that, the
balance per stock card / property cards should always reconcile with the
ledger cards of the accounting unit and other property records like
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE); and
c) the Municipal Mayor should cause for the conduct of physical count of PPE
annually and the preparation of the annual Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) which has to be submitted to the
concerned Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.

observations.managementAction:
The LGU rendered a list of its PPE as of December 31, 2016 but not in accordance with the prescribed format of the Report on Physical Count of PPE of the LGU. Only a list of the PPE without indicating therein the cost of each PPE and other particulars.
For CY 2017, only item D of our recommendations was implemented. Items A to C remained unimplemented.
observations.reason: According to the management, other priority projects, programs and activities of the LGU hinder their desire to comply with our recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LGU should fast track the making remittances of current dues to the GSIS. Further, the LGU should set-up a provision with corresponding appropriation equal to the probable balance of actual payables after reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution of the un-reconciled payables to GSIS.
Further, the LGU made a disclosure of the matter in the Notes to FS.
observations.managementAction: The LGU is now up-to-date in remittances of current dues to the GSIS. As to the un-reconciled account per audit finding, both agencies are still in the process of implementation as the LGU and the GSIS had not come up yet with the MOA for the settlement of the account. In addition, the LGU made a disclosure of the matter in the Notes to FS.
observations.reason: The LGU and the GSIS had not come up yet with the MOA for the settlement of the account. In addition, the LGU should determine whether its contingent liability with the GSIS had a probable certainty as to require recognition of a provision for contingent liability in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by: A. demonstrating leadership and by example; B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and C. upholding due compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the rendition of financial reports.
observations.managementAction:
For CY 2017, the LGU was able to submit nearly on time its monthly Reports of Checks Issued (RCIs) and Reports of Disbursements (RoDs) for audit except for Reports of Collections and Deposits (RCDs), Consolidated Reports of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAFs) and BRSs. It was our impression that rendition of RCD, CRAAF and BRS might not have been strictly enforced because the reports seemed to have less importance to the management considering that the LGU was highly dependent on the Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA).
Transactions involving local and other collections were quite minimal.
Further, both collecting and disbursing functions were being discharged by the Municipal Treasurer alone, giving her adding difficulty to prepare reports.
observations.reason: The LGU still failed to render on time other monthly reports, namely: RCD, CRAAF and BRS.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our AAR recommendation requiring top management to strictly implement necessary reforms for fiscal accountability by: A. demonstrating leadership by example; B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and C. maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: a. journals, b. ledgers, c. registries, and d. cash records.
observations.managementAction: The LGU was able to fairly comply with our audit recommendations especially its books of accounts, registries and cashbooks.
observations.reason: Other records such as ledger cards, property cards and RAAO for LDRRMF were still deficient.

observations.recommendation: We recommended  that the        Municipal       Accountant      should make     necessary       corrections in  the recording of|PPE by          transferring to|the general fund|books all assets|representing       constructions    that are fully  liquidated /  completed       pursuant to     Section 104 of  the NGAS for   LGUs so that   assets recorded|in the registry   of public      infrastructures|should then be  brought back to |the books to    starting the   year 2016.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant was newly hired.  She must have been yet at a stage of work familiarizat ion and orientation, not yet well-versed to some transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should strictly adhere to the mandates of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 and RA 10121 (PDRRM), specifically on accounting and reporting of LDRRMF such as the following: a separate Registry for Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) is properly maintained for LDRRMF; a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF is duly rendered; and all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE are transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” (Code 438) in the Trust Funds books.
observations.managementAction: The LGU was able to come up with a “GAD Plan and Budget”.
observations.reason: Per COA guideline, the “Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF” should be made on a monthly basis, not on a quarterly basis. In addition, management failed to maintain a separate RAAO. Moreover, the transfer of unexpended / unobligated balance of LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” (Code 438) in the Trust Funds books was not followed, instead it was accounted for under general fund forming part of the government equity.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should strictly comply with the provisions of PCW-NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01 in particular to the due rendition of GAD Plan and Budget as well as GAD Accomplishment Report. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should make appropriate disclosure on the nature of the amount of ₱(2,885,071.67) representing ‘prior period errors’ which was deducted from the beginning balance of the government equity. The Municipal Accountant should revisit pertinent journals / ledger to determine the nature / composition of ‘prior year errors’ and effect necessary adjustments, if any.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant was newly hired.  She must have been yet at a stage of work familiarizat ion and orientation, not yet well-versed to some transactions.

observations.recommendation:
Audit Recommendation
We recommended that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation of records between the LGU and the GSIS.  Further, the LGU should set-up a provision with corresponding appropriation equal to the probable balance of actual payables after reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution of the un-reconciled payables to GSIS.

Audit Recommendation
We recommended that top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by: a) demonstrating leadership by example; b) training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and c) upholding due compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the rendition of financial reports.

Audit Recommendation
We exhort the management to submit the financial documents and records within ten (10) working days of the ensuing month from the time the account should have been rendered and maintain a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: a. journals, b. ledgers, c. registries and d. cash records.

We recommended  that the LGU    should strictly   enforce the     provisions of   the New         Government      Accounting      System (NGAS)   Manual for LGUs   and the         Philippine      Public Sector   Accounting      Standard        (PPSAS), in     particular to   Sections 114 and|124 of the      Manual and PPSAS|17 which        mandates the    following:      the chief       accountant      should maintain |PPE Ledger Card |for each        category of PPE.|Such ledger     cards should    contain the     details of the  PPE account in  the inventory   control account |in the general  ledger;         the general     services officer| (GSO) or the    municipal       treasurer, as   the case maybe, |should maintain |stock cards and  |property cards  |for supplies and|PPE to account  |for the receipt |and disposition |of the same and,| that,, the      |balance per     |stock card /    |property cards  |should always   |reconcile with  |the ledger cards|of the          |accounting unit |and other       |property records|like            |Acknowledgement|Receipt for     |Equipment (ARE);| the local chief |executive should|cause for the   |conduct of      |physical count  |of PPE annually |and the         |preparation of  |the annual      |Report on the   |Physical Count  |of Property,    |Plant and       |Equipment       |(RPCPPE) which  |has to be       |submitted to the|concerned       |Auditor not     |later than      |January 31 of   |each year; and  |infrastructure  |assets should be|recognized in   |the books of    |accounts as PPE.|Those previously|recorded in the |Registries of   |Public Infrastructures (RPIs)|should then be  |brought back gradually to the books of accounts effective CY 2016 prompting further the need to update the RPIs to facilitate eventual transfer of records.
observations.managementAction: The LGU  rendered  submitted a list of its PPE as of December 31, 2016.
observations.reason: The prescribed format of the Report on Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) was not followed. Only a list of the PPE without indicating therein the cost of each PPE and other particulars.

observations.recommendation: We recommended  that management |should fully    disclose all   necessary       information in  the financial   statements      pursuant to the|mandates of the |PPSAS.  We     further         recommended that|the Municipal    Accountant      should account |for the nature  of the ‘payment |of expenses’ and|‘purchase /      construction of|PPE’ in the     amount of      ₱1,146,651.92  and            ₱1,897,752.73, |respectively, as |reflected in the |statement of cash|flows for the   year ended     December 31,   2015, and    rectify deficiency|in the books of |accounts, if any,|so to ensure     fair presentation|of statement of  |cash flows in the|future.

We recommended  that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation  of records between the LGU and the GSIS. Further, the LGU should set-up a provision with corresponding appropriation equal to the probable balance of actual payables after reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution of the unreconciled payables to GSIS.
observations.managementAction: The LGU and the GSIS had already come up with a Memorandu m of Understan ding (MOU) for the reconcili ation of their respectiv e records.
observations.reason: The LGU and the GSIS had not come up yet with a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) for the settlement of the account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by: a) demonstrating leadership by example; b) training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and c) upholding due compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the rendition of financial reports.
observations.managementAction: Periodic financial reports, i.e., disbursement reports, were being submitted quite early if compared to previous years.
observations.reason: Other reports, i.e., reports of collections and deposits (RCDs), bank reconciliati on statements (BRSs), consolidated report of accountability for accountable forms (CRAAFs), were being submitted late for audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by: a) demonstrating leadership by example; b) training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and c) upholding due compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the rendition of financial reports.
observations.managementAction: The LGU was able to maintain a complete set of books, registries and cashbooks.
observations.reason: Entries to said records were not kept up-to-date on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should strictly comply with the provisions of PCW-NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01 in particular to the due rendition of GAD Plan and Budget as well as GAD Accomplishment Report.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant was newly hired.  She must have been yet at a stage of work familiarizat ion and orientation, not yet well-versed to some transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should strictly enforce the operational mandates of the Office of the Municipal Assessor in particular to the assessment of real property for the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to come up with a realistic tax valuation of real property for use as valid input in the books and serving as basis in enforcing collections of RPT and SEF receivables.
